vulnerabilities.description: Sensitive data stored in plaintext JSON files without encryption. Any API keys, tokens, or secrets passed to memory.set() will be persisted unencrypted in .claude-flow/data/memory.json, readable by any process with file access.

Temporary files created with default permissions. writeFileSync() creates temp files readable by other users on multi-user systems. Should use chmod 0600 or equivalent to restrict access to temp files containing sensitive GitHub body content.
Session context data stored in plaintext. The 'context' object in session.json is saved unencrypted. If this stores authentication tokens or sensitive data, it poses a risk on multi-user systems.
Error messages may leak system information. The catch block logs full error objects that could contain file paths or system details. Consider logging only sanitized error messages to console.

Potential reflected XSS if backend doesn't validate 'q' parameter. While encodeURIComponent() is correctly used, the backend /vault endpoint must validate and sanitize the 'q' parameter to prevent XSS attacks.

recommendations: Encrypt sensitive data at rest in memory.js using a simple encryption library (e.g., crypto with AES-256-GCM) before storing to disk
Restrict temp file permissions to owner only: use writeFileSync() with mode 0o600 in github-safe.js line 68
Implement input validation on the /vault backend endpoint to sanitize the 'q' parameter against XSS attacks
Add type checking and validation for session context data — reject storing sensitive tokens/passwords
Review package.json and package-lock.json for vulnerable dependencies using 'npm audit'
Add error logging sanitization to prevent leaking system paths or stack traces to console
Use environment variables with proper secret management (e.g., never store secrets in .claude-flow/data/)
Verify all API endpoints validate CORS and authentication before responding with sensitive data
